meta.title: Section 122 Tariffs at 150-Day Expiry (July 2026)
meta.question: What happens to the Trump administration's Section 122 10% universal tariff when it reaches its 150-day statutory limit in late July 2026?
meta.resolution: Observable: White House action or documented inaction by July 23, 2026 (150 days from Feb 24 effective date). Resolution criteria: (a) tariff expires with no successor = H1; (b) new proclamation issues at ~10% using different authority = H2; (c) new proclamation/action at higher rate or expanded scope = H3; (d) bilateral deal(s) announced covering >=30% of affected import value with reduced rates = H4. Partial-deal-plus-continuation counts as H4 if rollback is material.

_notes.statutory_cap: Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 (19 U.S.C. 2132) authorizes up to 15% tariff surcharges for balance-of-payments purposes, but caps them at 150 days unless Congress extends. The 150-day clock runs from Feb 24 2026, expiring approximately July 23 2026.
_notes.ieepa_context: SCOTUS struck IEEPA-based tariffs in Feb 2026. Administration pivoted to Section 122 as the available statutory authority. Section 301 investigations into EU/Mexico/China launched simultaneously suggest intent to build alternative legal basis.
_notes.prior_basis: Priors reflect: (1) base rate of Trump-era trade escalation over negotiation; (2) active Section 301 investigations as pipeline for escalation; (3) bilateral negotiation framing (H4) as stated goal but 3.5-month runway is tight for complex deals; (4) near-zero probability of clean expiry given political costs.
_notes.uncertainty: Key uncertainty is whether Section 301 investigations can produce actionable tariff authority before the Section 122 clock runs out, and whether any bilateral deal reaches material scope. Congress extending Section 122 is possible but historically rare.

LEGAL._note: Legal challenges or court rulings limiting authority push toward H1

DIPLOMATIC._note: Diplomatic signals (deal progress, partner concessions) push toward H4

H1.label: Expires as scheduled — no replacement, no extension
H1.description: The tariff lapses on ~July 23, 2026 with no successor action. Administration makes no new proclamation, Congress does not authorize extension, Section 301 investigations have not yet produced usable tariff authority. Politically very costly — would be framed as capitulation. Requires either a court order forcing expiry or a deliberate policy reversal. Base rate for clean expiry given current posture: low.
H1._prior_note: 7% reflects near-impossibility of deliberate no-action given political signaling. Only fires if legal constraint (court order) or unexpected political reversal.

H2.label: Maintained at same ~10% rate via new authority
H2.description: Administration issues a new proclamation at roughly the same 10% rate using a different statutory basis — most likely Section 301 authority once investigations conclude, or a Section 232 national security finding, or a new IEEPA invocation with a different legal framing. Rate stays flat, scope roughly unchanged. This is the 'hold the line' outcome: no escalation, no deal, but tariffs continue.
H2._prior_note: 18% reflects plausibility as a fallback if deals stall and escalation seems risky, but narrower than H3/H4 because straight-rate maintenance is strategically inert and doesn't match the administration's pattern of using expiry moments for leverage.

H3.label: Expanded — higher rate and/or broader product scope
H3.description: At or before expiry, administration announces escalation: higher tariff rate (above 10%), expanded country coverage, expanded product scope, or a new Section 301 tariff regime (historically 25%+ on targeted categories) that replaces the universal surcharge with a more aggressive targeted structure. New Section 301 investigations into EU, Mexico, and China are the most direct pipeline. A 25% targeted tariff on EU autos or Chinese tech goods would qualify as H3 even if the universal 10% lapses.
H3._prior_note: 33% reflects the administration's track record of using tariff deadlines as escalation moments rather than resolution moments, plus active Section 301 pipeline. H3 is the modal outcome if bilateral deals fail to materialize.

H4.label: Partial rollback — bilateral deal(s) reduce tariffs on >=30% of affected import value
H4.description: One or more bilateral trade agreements or framework deals (EU, Mexico, Japan, UK, Canada, or others) are announced before or at the July 23 deadline, resulting in materially reduced tariff rates on a significant share of the ~$1.2T import base. Does not require a comprehensive agreement — sector deals (e.g., EU autos, Mexican agricultural goods) that cover >=30% of affected volume qualify. May be accompanied by continuation of tariffs on non-deal partners. This is the stated preferred outcome of the administration and trading partners.
H4._prior_note: 42% reflects: (1) bilateral deal framing as stated policy goal; (2) three active negotiating tracks (EU, Mexico/Canada, China-adjacent); (3) both sides have economic incentives before US midterm season; BUT (4) discount heavily because 3.5 months is tight for complex deals, and partial-deal announcements often get classified as 'framework' without tariff relief. Set as modal outcome but note high uncertainty.

section301Pipeline.label: Section 301 Investigation Progress
section301Pipeline.value: Investigations launched Apr 2026 into EU, Mexico, China. Typically 6-12 months to completion; unlikely to produce tariff authority before July 23.
section301Pipeline.description: Section 301 investigations require notice, comment period, and USTR determination before tariffs can issue. Tracks whether any investigation is fast-tracked or whether findings precede the July 23 deadline. A completed 301 determination before July 23 would enable H3 escalation with new legal footing.

bilateralNegotiations.label: Bilateral Deal Progress
bilateralNegotiations.value: EU: framework talks reported, no tariff relief offered yet. Mexico: USMCA review ongoing. China: no direct talks. UK: advanced.
bilateralNegotiations.description: Tracks progress of bilateral negotiations. Key threshold for H4: formal announcement of tariff reduction on >=30% of affected import value. A 'framework agreement' without tariff relief does not qualify. Monitors: EU-US trade talks, USMCA/Mexico negotiations, UK-US deal, Japan-US.

legalChallenges.label: Legal/Judicial Constraints on Section 122 Authority
legalChallenges.value: No known active challenges to Section 122 authority as of April 2026. SCOTUS IEEPA ruling limited executive tariff authority but did not address Section 122.
legalChallenges.description: Tracks whether courts have limited or enjoined Section 122 authority. A court ruling striking Section 122 would force H1 or require Congressional action. Also tracks Congressional legislation (extension or repeal).

importVolumeImpact.label: Import Volume Response
importVolumeImpact.value: Frontloading reported in Q1 2026; underlying demand suppression beginning. No official import volume data post-Feb 24.
importVolumeImpact.description: Tracks how importers are responding to the tariff. Sustained frontloading suggests trading partners expect continuation (H2/H3). Sharp import collapse suggests economic pressure building toward deal (H4) or expiry (H1). Measured via Census Bureau monthly trade data.

_design_rules: Each indicator is OBSERVABLE — an action, data point, or published document, not an opinion or prediction
Each has a clear fired/not-fired threshold
Anti-indicators explicitly reduce specific hypothesis probabilities
Governor flags indicators as FIRED only when threshold is clearly met

tier1_critical.desc: Formal bilateral trade agreement announced covering >=30% of affected $1.2T import volume, with explicit tariff rate reductions. Must include USTR press release and partner government confirmation. 'Framework' or 'talks ongoing' do not qualify.

Presidential proclamation or USTR notice issued announcing tariff rate above 10% or expanded product/country scope BEFORE or AT the July 23 deadline. Must be a formal Federal Register action, not a statement of intent.
July 23, 2026 passes with no White House proclamation, no Congressional extension, no new tariff authority issued. Observable via Federal Register absence and White House press releases.

USTR issues formal Section 301 tariff determination before July 23 that replaces or extends the universal surcharge with targeted product tariffs. Must be a signed Federal Register notice with effective date.
tier1_critical.posteriorEffect: H3 +20pp if rate >10%; H2 +15pp if rate ~10%; H4 -10pp (suggests deal less likely)

tier1_critical.desc: Congress passes legislation authorizing extension of Section 122 tariff authority beyond 150 days, signed by President. Observable: bill passage and presidential signature.
tier1_critical.note: Historically rare. Congress has not used this authority since the 1970s. Would likely be attached to a larger trade bill.

tier2_strong.desc: EU-US trade talks reach ministerial level with explicit agenda item for tariff relief before July 23. Must be documented via EU Commission or USTR official statement, not media speculation.
USTR releases Request for Comments or formal notice of intent to negotiate bilateral tariff reduction with a specific partner (EU, Japan, UK, Mexico). Procedural step required before deal.
USTR announces accelerated Section 301 timeline (completion before July 2026) for any of the active investigations into EU, Mexico, or China. Must cite expedited schedule in official notice.

Trump or senior official (USTR, Treasury Secretary) makes on-record statement framing July deadline as 'deal or escalation' choice, explicitly naming a target country and timeline. Distinguishable from routine rhetoric by specificity: named country, named date, named rate.
tier2_strong.note: Rhetoric only — tag as RHETORIC, not EVENT. Does not fire indicator but shifts priors modestly.
tier2_strong.posteriorEffect: H3 +5pp or H4 +5pp depending on framing; H1 -3pp in either case

tier2_strong.desc: Monthly Census Bureau trade data shows imports from tariffed partners down >=15% from pre-tariff baseline for two consecutive months. Observable: monthly trade release from Census.
tier2_strong.posteriorEffect: H4 +7pp (economic pressure toward deal), H3 -4pp (escalation less likely if damage severe)

tier2_strong.desc: Industry group or foreign government files legal challenge to Section 122 authority in federal court, with preliminary injunction sought. Observable: court filing docket.
tier2_strong.note: SCOTUS IEEPA precedent makes a challenge plausible. A successful PI would force H1.

tier2_strong.desc: China announces retaliatory tariff measures specifically in response to Section 122 (not pre-existing actions). Observable: MOFCOM announcement.
tier2_strong.posteriorEffect: H3 +8pp (escalatory cycle), H4 -6pp (China-track deal less likely)

tier3_suggestive.desc: Trump job approval rises >=3pp in a national poll during a week featuring tariff-related news, suggesting political upside to the tariff stance. Observable: FiveThirtyEight or RealClearPolitics tracker.
tier3_suggestive.note: Approval correlation with tariff policy is weak but worth tracking as a political incentive signal.

tier3_suggestive.desc: Trading partner files formal WTO dispute settlement request against Section 122 tariffs. Observable: WTO dispute panel registry.
tier3_suggestive.note: WTO complaints move slowly and historically have not deterred US tariff policy, but filing signals credible partner intent.

tier3_suggestive.desc: BLS CPI report shows >=0.3pp monthly increase in goods categories dominated by tariffed imports (apparel, electronics, consumer goods). Observable: BLS monthly release.
NFIB Small Business Optimism Index falls below 90 two consecutive months, citing tariff costs as primary concern. Observable: NFIB monthly release.
tier3_suggestive.posteriorEffect: H4 +3pp, H1 +2pp (domestic pressure to end tariffs)

tier3_suggestive.desc: EU publishes formal list of US products for retaliatory tariffs, with implementation contingent on Section 122 continuation. Observable: EU Official Journal or Commission announcement.
Senate confirms new USTR (if vacancy) or USTR publicly commits to bilateral deal timeline naming a specific partner. Observable: Senate vote or USTR press conference.
tier3_suggestive.posteriorEffect: H4 +3pp if deal-framed, H3 +3pp if escalation-framed

anti_indicators.desc: Trump or senior official makes unambiguous public statement that tariffs WILL continue past July 23 in some form (renewal, escalation, or deal). Observable: White House press release or on-record quote.
anti_indicators.note: Any positive statement about post-July tariff policy reduces H1 probability. H1 requires near-total absence of successor action.
anti_indicators.lr_migration_warning: design prior drift >30% — review before next firing

anti_indicators.desc: Formal bilateral deal announced OR new tariff proclamation at rate substantially different from 10%. H2 requires rate continuity; any material rate change (up or down) removes H2.
Bilateral deal covering >=30% of affected import volume announced, AND no new escalation proclamation issued. Successful deal-making forecloses the escalation track.

New tariff proclamation at higher rate (>12%) issued, explicitly on products covered by active Section 301 investigations, WITHOUT parallel deal announcement. Escalation proclamation without deal component forecloses H4.
anti_indicators.lr_migration_warning: design prior drift >30% — review before next firing

actorModel.description: Decision-theoretic framework: who makes the decisions that drive the Section 122 expiry outcome?

trump.name: Donald Trump (President)
trump.role: Primary decision authority. Tariff policy is managed personally; USTR and Treasury operate as implementers, not independent actors.
trump.decisionStyle: Deal-framing: announces pressure, then claims victory. Prefers visible wins over substantive ones. Uses deadlines as leverage rather than hard constraints. Has reversed tariff positions rapidly when presented with market downturns or coalition pressure.
biases: Loss aversion on perceived capitulation — will not let July 23 look like a defeat
Market-as-approval-signal: sustained S&P decline accelerates deal-seeking (H4)
Personal grievance framing: EU and China treated differently from 'friends' (UK, Japan)
Transactionalism over rules-based order: bilateral deals preferred over multilateral frameworks
filters: Section 122 statutory cap is a hard legal constraint — cannot be extended by proclamation alone
Post-IEEPA SCOTUS ruling limits executive tariff authority generally; any new Section 301 action must be procedurally defensible
Treasury/Commerce provide economic cover narratives; if they signal recession risk, that filters toward H4
overrides: Geopolitical crisis (e.g., China-Taiwan, Iran-Hormuz) could override trade calculation entirely
Stock market crash >=15% from April 2026 levels would accelerate H4 regardless of other indicators
Personal business interest or foreign policy quid pro quo could produce surprise bilateral deal

ustr.name: USTR (US Trade Representative)
ustr.role: Executes trade policy, runs Section 301 investigations, negotiates bilateral deals. Has institutional interest in procedural legitimacy.
ustr.decisionStyle: Institutional, process-oriented. Moves on Section 301 via formal notice-and-comment; cannot fast-track below statutory minimums without legal exposure.
biases: Prefers tariff continuation to expiry — expiry means loss of leverage in ongoing negotiations
Bilateral deal preference aligns with institutional mandate
filters: Section 301 timelines are procedurally fixed at minimum 12 months for full investigation; any sub-July completion requires waiver or pre-existing work product
Federal Register publication deadlines: final action requires 30+ days notice minimum
overrides: Presidential direction to fast-track can accelerate some procedural steps
Emergency national security finding could bypass some procedural requirements

congress.name: US Congress (Senate Finance + House Ways and Means)
congress.role: Can extend Section 122 authority beyond 150 days via legislation. Can also constrain tariff authority via appropriations riders.
congress.decisionStyle: Institutionally slow. Extension requires bipartisan support in Senate. Republican majority broadly supports tariff goals but may resist blank-check extension. Democrats have mixed views (labor protectionism vs. consumer cost concerns).
biases: Midterm-year sensitivity (2026): members in swing districts will be alert to consumer price impact
Farm-state Republicans sensitive to retaliatory agricultural tariffs (EU, China, Mexico)
filters: Statutory extension requires floor time and votes — unlikely unless tied to must-pass legislation
Any extension bill is subject to amendment process that could add conditions
overrides: A must-pass vehicle (NDAA, continuing resolution) could carry a tariff extension rider

eu.name: European Union (Commission, DG Trade)
eu.role: Largest single affected trading partner by volume. Active in bilateral negotiations. Has retaliation authority and is preparing contingency lists.
eu.decisionStyle: Consensual, slow-moving internally. Commission can negotiate but requires member state approval for binding agreements. France and Germany have divergent interests (agriculture vs. autos).
biases: Prefers rules-based resolution but willing to make bilateral concessions to protect auto sector
French agricultural sensitivities constrain concession space
German auto industry has strong lobby for bilateral deal
filters: EU cannot do a binding deal in 3.5 months without extraordinary political will — a framework or 'standstill' agreement is more realistic
EU retaliatory measures require QMV approval and have political costs
overrides: Threat of 25% US auto tariffs (Section 232 or 301) is the most powerful EU deal-forcing mechanism

china.name: People's Republic of China (MOFCOM, State Council)
china.role: Largest source of US tariff revenue under prior Section 301 regime. Active Section 301 investigation. Has retaliation toolkit.
china.decisionStyle: Strategic patience. China is unlikely to make major concessions under 3.5-month pressure. May offer tactical concessions on specific goods to manage escalation without appearing to capitulate.
biases: Face-saving: any deal must be frameable domestically as mutual rather than coerced
Long-term decoupling strategy means China is building alternative markets regardless
filters: MOFCOM requires approval from State Council for major tariff concessions
Ongoing tech decoupling limits scope of possible deal
overrides: US-China deal is lowest-probability bilateral track in this timeframe — weight China-track heavily toward H3 if deals are expected

methodology: ACTIONS OVER RHETORIC — proclamations, Federal Register notices, and signed agreements move posteriors; press conferences and statements do not
TAG EVERYTHING — Trump statements = RHETORIC until followed by Federal Register action; tag separately
DON'T FRONT-RUN — set tripwires on specific documents (FR notices, USTR filings) and let publication confirm
DEAL ANATOMY — a 'deal' must specify: (a) named partner, (b) tariff rate reduction, (c) implementation date, (d) both parties confirm. 'Framework' and 'talks ongoing' are not deals.
PROCEDURAL CALENDAR — Section 301 cannot complete in <12 months without extraordinary action; track USTR procedural calendar explicitly
SOCIALIZATION DETECTION — if USTR starts signaling 'targeted' vs. 'universal' tariff framing, that's narrative prep for H3 transition

evidenceLog.text: SCOTUS strikes IEEPA-based tariffs (Learning Resources v. Trump). Same day, Trump issues Section 122 proclamation: 10% universal import surcharge effective Feb 24. 150-day clock starts.
evidenceLog.source: Supreme Court ruling + White House Proclamation, Feb 20 2026
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: Founding event. Establishes the H1-H4 framing.
evidenceLog.note: Section 122 had never been used to impose tariffs before. Novel legal territory.

evidenceLog.text: Trump announces via social media intent to raise Section 122 rate to 15% (statutory maximum). No formal proclamation issued.
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: Mild H3 signal but RHETORIC only — no Federal Register action. Do not move posteriors on announcement alone.
evidenceLog.note: Tagged RHETORIC per framework rules. Social media intent ≠ formal action.

evidenceLog.text: Treasury Secretary Bessent confirms intent to raise to 15% on CNBC. Also references ongoing Section 301 investigations and Section 232 studies as successor frameworks during 150-day window.
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: Strengthens H2/H3 pipeline — successor authority actively being built. Still no formal action.
evidenceLog.note: Bessent statement is highest-ranking official confirmation of escalation intent AND successor framework preparation. CNBC base trust 0.65.

evidenceLog.text: 24 state attorneys general (led by Oregon, Arizona, California, New York, Vermont) file suit at US Court of International Trade challenging Section 122 surcharge. Case No. 1:26-cv-01472. States argue balance-of-payments requirement not met.
evidenceLog.source: CIT filing, Oregon et al. v. Trump, March 5 2026
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: H1 +signal: legal pathway to injunction now exists. t2_court_challenge_filed indicator partially relevant.
evidenceLog.note: 24 states is the broadest state coalition against Section 122. Matches indicator t2_court_challenge_filed.

evidenceLog.text: Liberty Justice Center files second suit (Burlap and Barrel v. Trump, Case No. 1:26-cv-01606) on behalf of spice importer and toy company. Assigned to same 3-judge CIT panel (Barnett, Kelly, Stanceu).
evidenceLog.source: CIT filing, Burlap and Barrel v. Trump, March 9 2026
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: Reinforces H1 legal pathway. Two parallel challenges increase judicial engagement.
evidenceLog.note: Both cases consolidated before same panel for April 10 oral arguments.

evidenceLog.text: As of March 30, rate remains 10%. No formal proclamation raising to 15% has been issued despite repeated announcements. 15% rhetoric has not translated to Federal Register action.
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: Mildly reduces H3 probability — if escalation were imminent, proclamation would have issued. But 15% still possible before July.
evidenceLog.note: Gap between rhetoric and action is itself informative. 36 days of announced intent with no formal action.

evidenceLog.text: Reuters reports Trump-Xi summit planned for mid-May in Beijing. Trade war framing: China responded with reciprocal investigations. Prior Busan meeting produced truce (tariff trim + fentanyl + soybean purchases). May summit is highest-level bilateral trade engagement since Section 122.
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. H4-directional but no indicator fired: t1_bilateral_deal_announced requires formal deal covering >=30% volume. A scheduled summit is not a deal. Posteriors HOLD — summit is precondition for deal, not deal itself. Monitor May outcome.
evidenceLog.note: Reuters effectiveTrust 0.9643 from source_db. Summit delayed from original date due to Iran war.

evidenceLog.text: CIT oral arguments held April 10-11. Three-judge panel (Barnett, Kelly, Stanceu) sharply questioned both sides for 3+ hours. Key issue: whether 'balance-of-payments deficits' in 1974 statute covers trade deficits. Awkward for government: DOJ argued last year Section 122 had 'no obvious application' to trade deficits. Awkward for challengers: CIT itself wrote Section 122 was available when striking IEEPA tariffs.
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: Indicator t2_court_challenge_filed now ACTIVE (arguments heard). Trade lawyer (King & Spalding) predicts court will defer to president given temporary nature. If so, H1 legal pathway weakens.
evidenceLog.note: AP effectiveTrust 0.99. Expert assessment: 'stunned if challengers prevail' — but expert opinion is ANALYSIS, not outcome.

evidenceLog.text: March 2026 CPI: headline +0.9% m/m, 3.3% annual (highest since April 2024). Energy +10.9% (Iran war driven). But core CPI +0.2% m/m, 2.6% annual — both below forecast. Tariff impact visible in apparel (+1%) and airline fares (+2.7%).
evidenceLog.source: BLS CPI release via CNBC, April 10 2026
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: Matches indicator t3_cpi_spike_tariff_attributed partially — goods categories show tariff pass-through but headline is energy-dominated. Core below forecast reduces political pressure to remove tariffs on inflation grounds.
evidenceLog.note: CNBC base trust 0.65 but reporting BLS primary data. Effective trust for DATA tag: BLS primary source.

evidenceLog.text: One-year anniversary of 'Liberation Day' tariffs. NPR/CNBC assessments: tariffs have not produced promised economic boom. Tax Foundation estimates average household cost $700/year. Trade deficit not meaningfully altered.
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: Background context. Political pressure from economic impact could favor H4 (deal) over H3 (escalation). But Trump has historically doubled down despite adverse data.
evidenceLog.note: Multiple-source convergence on economic assessment. Tagged ANALYSIS because it involves interpretation of aggregate data.

evidenceLog.text: White House reports 18 bilateral framework trade agreements completed covering more than half of global GDP as result of Section 122 tariffs. CIT three-judge panel heard oral arguments April 10-11 on 23-state legal challenge.
evidenceLog.note: Matches indicator t2_court_challenge_filed (tier2 strong). Pre-committed effect: H1 +8pp, H2 -3pp, H3 -3pp. R_t is RUNAWAY — this topic is evidence-starved, even tangential intel has value. Source trust: 50% (moderate).

GOVERNANCE WARNING on bayesian_update: stale_evidence. Reason: Status quo at 10% with active legal challenge but no rollback — supports H2; expansion-to-15% threat keeps H3 alive; bilateral-deal H4 path neither advanced nor closed.

Section 122 tariffs effective Feb 24, 2026 expire July 24, 2026 absent congressional extension; 24 AGs and governors filed CIT challenge Mar 5; trade experts argue legal grounds for invocation are weak. No congressional extension legislation has advanced as of late April.
GOVERNANCE WARNING on bayesian_update: stale_evidence. Reason: Pending CIT lawsuit + absent extension bill + weak legal grounds elevate the expiry hypothesis above its current 0.005 floor.

tariff_rate.label: Current Section 122 Tariff Rate (%)
tariff_rate._note: Tracks rate of active tariff. Will change if H2/H3 occur. Resets to 0 if H1 occurs.

days_remaining.label: Days Until Section 122 Expiry
days_remaining._note: Counts down from 150-day clock starting Feb 24. At zero, statutory authority lapses unless extended.

import_volume_index.label: Tariffed Import Volume Index (100 = pre-tariff baseline)
import_volume_index._note: Monthly Census Bureau trade data. Proxy for economic pressure. Sharp decline = deal pressure or demand destruction.

us_eu_trade_talks.label: EU-US Trade Talks Status
us_eu_trade_talks.value: Technical-level talks ongoing, no tariff relief offered
us_eu_trade_talks._note: Categories: No talks / Technical talks / Ministerial talks / Framework agreement / Binding deal

section301_status.label: Section 301 Investigation Status (EU/Mexico/China)
section301_status.value: Investigations launched April 2026; comment period pending
section301_status._note: Tracks whether any 301 investigation is on track to complete before July 23. Standard timeline: 12 months from launch.

brent_crude.label: Brent Crude ($/bbl)
brent_crude._note: Indirect signal: oil price reflects macro conditions that affect trade deal urgency.

sp500_level.label: S&P 500 Level
sp500_level._note: Trump uses S&P as approval proxy. Sustained decline >=15% from peak accelerates H4 (deal-seeking).

cpi_goods.label: CPI Goods Month-over-Month Change (%)
cpi_goods._note: BLS monthly release. Tariff pass-through signal. Sustained >=0.3pp monthly increase adds deal pressure.

trump_approval.label: Trump Job Approval (%)
trump_approval._note: RCP or 538 tracker. Declining approval during tariff period signals political cost.

watchpoints.desc: Federal Register: any USTR or White House tariff action published before July 23
watchpoints.note: Primary resolution signal. Any FR notice is a tier-1 event.

watchpoints.desc: USTR press releases on Section 301 investigation timelines
EU-US trade talks official communiques (EU Commission trade section)
Census Bureau monthly trade data (goods imports by partner)
WTO dispute settlement registry for US Section 122 cases
Court PACER / federal district court filings for Section 122 legal challenges
watchpoints.note: PI filing = t2_court_challenge_filed fires. Monitor Court of International Trade specifically.

watchpoints.desc: Congressional floor schedule (Senate Finance, House Ways and Means) for tariff extension legislation
watchpoints.note: Any markup or floor vote on tariff extension = t1_congress_extension pipeline.

searchQueries: USTR Section 301 investigation EU Mexico China 2026
US EU trade deal tariff reduction 2026
US trade deficit April 2026 census bureau
Court of International Trade Section 122 challenge 2026

expiredHypotheses.label: Expires as scheduled — no replacement, no extension
Maintained at same ~10% rate via new authority
Expanded — higher rate and/or broader product scope
Partial rollback — bilateral deal(s) reduce tariffs on >=30% of affected import value

dependencies._docs: Cross-topic dependency declarations. Upstream topics whose posteriors this topic's assumptions depend on.

upstream.reason: Recession increases political pressure for tariff rollback (H4 deal) or forces Congress to let Section 122 expire (H1). Recession probability shift affects H1/H4 balance.

predictionScoring._docs: Calibration infrastructure. Snapshots auto-created by engine on each posterior update. Record outcome when topic resolves, then compute_calibration_report() for Brier analysis.

outcomes.label: Expires as scheduled — no replacement, no extension
Maintained at same ~10% rate via new authority
Expanded — higher rate and/or broader product scope
Partial rollback — bilateral deal(s) reduce tariffs on >=30% of affected import value

BLUE.reasoning: WTO dispute-settlement filing is a textbook institutional resolution mechanism activating.

GRAY.reasoning: WTO dispute filing is cleanly observable.

RED.reasoning: WTO formal protest on tariff expansion names retaliation risk sharply. RED-coherent.

VIOLET.reasoning: Names at least one specific decision-maker controlling the outcome; incentive is implicit but traceable.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF expanded to broader scope, THEN affected US importers file ≥500 new Exclusion Requests within 60 days
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: USTR official count of filed exclusion requests ≥500 from announcement date to 60 days after

AMBER.reasoning: 500+ exclusion request surge is a threshold cascade indicating importer defection from the tariff regime.

BLUE.reasoning: Exclusion-request process is a formal USTR absorption channel for tariff stress.

RED.reasoning: 500+ exclusion requests captures importer distress cascade. Good.

VIOLET.reasoning: Names multiple specific decision-makers (agencies, officials, firms, states) and ties the outcome to their choices. Actor-incentive traceability strong.
